title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands

"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

The choropleth map displays global excess nitrogen from croplands for the year 2009, utilizing a color scale ranging from light yellow for 100,000 tonnes to dark brown for 25 million tonnes. The highest concentrations, represented by the darkest brown shades indicating values between 10 million and 25 million tonnes, are visible in China, India, and the United States. Western Europe, including France and Germany, along with Brazil and parts of Southeast Asia such as Indonesia, appear in medium-to-dark orange tones, corresponding to the 1 million to 10 million tonne range. Canada, Australia, and Argentina are shaded in lighter orange, falling within the 250,000 to 1 million tonne band. Large portions of Africa, including the Sahara region and central areas, are colored in pale yellow, signifying levels between 100,000 and 250,000 tonnes. Russia and several nations in northern Africa and the Middle East are colored blue, denoting regions where nitrogen mining occurs rather than excess accumulation. Greenland and a few other territories are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data availability. The visual distribution highlights a distinct concentration of high excess nitrogen values in major agricultural economies across Asia and North America, while vast areas of the southern hemisphere and northern latitudes show significantly lower surplus levels or net nutrient depletion.
